input.userRequest: Recommend how much tracing and retry behavior to keep for the next demo run.

messages.content: You are Briefing Genie. Return a short briefing with source citations.

User request: Recommend how much tracing and retry behavior to keep for the next demo run.

Source packet: Cost And Latency Budget Packet

Packet summary: Run data, finance guidance, and user feedback about trace depth, retries, and demo responsiveness.

Sources:

[S1] Run telemetry
Type: telemetry report
The candidate prompt reduced unsupported claims but increased median token usage by 11%. Latency improved by 6% because fewer generations needed manual reruns, even though each retained trace is longer.

Telemetry compares the candidate prompt with the baseline over the visible eval set. Token usage rose because the candidate retained more claim-level evidence, but fewer outputs needed manual reruns. The latency improvement came from fewer repeated attempts, not from shorter single generations.

[S2] Finance guidance
Type: finance note
Finance approved up to a 1.15x cost ratio for demo-quality eval runs if the report shows why extra tokens reduce review time. Anything above that threshold needs a smaller case batch or stricter retry cap.

Finance accepted a 1.15x ceiling for demo-quality runs because better traces can reduce reviewer time. The approval is conditional: if the run exceeds the threshold, the team should reduce batch size, cap retries, or summarize traces more aggressively. The note does not approve unlimited trace retention.

[S3] Reviewer feedback
Type: reviewer feedback
Reviewers said long traces are useful only when they are summarized next to the claim that failed. Raw provider payloads alone slowed review because people had to search for the relevant evidence.

Reviewers liked traces when they were attached to the failed claim and cited source. Raw payload dumps slowed them down because they had to search for the relevant turn. The requested improvement is a short trace summary near each evaluator finding.

[S4] Demo rehearsal
Type: rehearsal note
The live demo felt responsive when single briefings finished under 10 seconds and eval progress showed the current case. Stakeholders were tolerant of longer full runs if progress and artifact paths updated continuously.

The rehearsal note separates product responsiveness from lab-run duration. A single briefing felt live-demo safe under 10 seconds. Full eval runs could take longer if the UI showed the current case, elapsed time, and artifact path so observers understood progress.

[S5] Retry policy draft
Type: policy draft
The retry policy draft proposes a small retry cap for demo eval runs. It allows one automatic retry for transient provider errors and requires a manifest entry when retries change cost or latency. The policy rejects silent repeated generations because they make cost harder to explain during review.

[S6] Trace summary mock
Type: trace mock
The trace summary mock shows a short explanation beside each failed claim: selected source ids, missing evidence, retry count, and final output path. Reviewers preferred that mock to raw provider payloads. The mock keeps detailed traces on disk while making the dashboard readable during a live demo.

output.title: Recommendation: Tracing depth and retry behavior for next demo run
output.summary: Keep claim-level trace retention but surface only short, per-claim summaries in the UI; keep detailed payloads on disk. Use a tight retry cap: one automatic retry only for transient provider errors, with explicit manifest logging. This balances reviewer usefulness and demo responsiveness while staying within the 1.15x cost ceiling.

claims.text: Candidate prompt increased median token usage by 11% but improved latency by 6% due to fewer manual reruns (not shorter single generations).

Finance will accept up to a 1.15x cost ratio for demo-quality runs if extra tokens demonstrably reduce review time; above that requires smaller batch size, stricter retry caps, or more aggressive trace summarization.
Reviewers find long traces useful only when summarized next to the failing claim; raw provider payload dumps slow review.
Demo responsiveness target: single briefings should finish under ~10 seconds; longer full runs are acceptable if progress (current case, elapsed time, artifact path) updates continuously.
Retry draft: allow one automatic retry for transient provider errors; require a manifest entry when retries affect cost/latency; reject silent repeated generations.

openQuestions: What is the current cost ratio for the demo run configuration (with claim-level traces + summaries) relative to baseline, and does it stay within the 1.15x ceiling?
How are “transient provider errors” detected/defined in the current stack (error codes vs timeouts), to ensure retries don’t mask systematic failures?
Do we need to reduce case batch size to guarantee single-briefing latency under 10 seconds during the live demo, or is UI progress feedback sufficient?
Where will the detailed traces be stored and how will artifact paths be exposed live (to satisfy rehearsal expectations)?
output.recommendation:
Tracing: Keep claim-level evidence retention (so evaluators can point to sources) but do not display raw provider payloads by default. Instead, show a short per-claim trace summary beside each failing claim including selected source ids, what evidence is missing, retry count, and final output path; keep full detailed traces on disk for post-demo drill-down. This matches reviewer preferences and keeps the demo dashboard readable. (S3, S6)

Retries: Adopt a strict demo retry cap: one automatic retry only for transient provider errors; no silent repeated generations. Log retry events in a manifest whenever they change cost/latency so cost is explainable in review. If costs trend above 1.15x, tighten further by reducing batch size and/or summarizing traces more aggressively rather than adding retries. (S5, S2)

Demo UX guardrails: Optimize for <10s per single briefing where possible, but rely on continuous progress indicators (current case, elapsed time, artifact path) to maintain perceived responsiveness for longer full runs. (S4)

pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-23
